1.synthesis:
Psalm 79 is a communal lament over the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple — the most catastrophic event in Israel's theological history. The opening triple horror — 'O God, the nations have come into your inheritance; they have defiled your holy temple; they have laid Jerusalem in ruins. They have given the bodies of your servants to the birds of the heavens for food, the flesh of your faithful to the beasts of the earth. They have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them' (vv. 1–3) — describes not merely military defeat but covenant catastrophe: the sanctuary defiled, YHWH's inheritance invaded, and his servants left unburied (the ultimate shame in the ancient world). The repeated 'your': 'your inheritance,' 'your holy temple,' 'your servants,' 'your faithful' — frames the entire disaster as an assault on YHWH himself.

The crisis of divine wrath is addressed directly in vv. 5–8: 'How long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? Will your jealousy burn like fire? Pour out your anger on the nations that do not know you, and on the kingdoms that do not call upon your name! For they have devoured Jacob and laid waste his habitation... Do not remember against us our former iniquities; let your compassion come speedily to meet us, for we are brought very low.' The psalm does not avoid the theological complexity: YHWH's wrath is burning against both the nations (for their assault on his sanctuary) and Israel (whose 'former iniquities' precipitated the disaster). The appeal is not for unqualified deliverance but for YHWH's compassion to override the deserved judgment.

The theological argument of vv. 9–10 shifts to YHWH's own honor: 'Help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name; deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name's sake! Why should the nations say, “Where is their God?”' The appeal is based not on Israel's merit but on YHWH's own reputation among the nations. The nations' taunt — 'Where is their God?' — is the specific theological threat: if YHWH fails to act, the nations will conclude that he is absent or powerless, which dishonors him. The petition is therefore simultaneously for Israel's deliverance and for YHWH's vindication. The vow of v. 13 closes the psalm with a promise of praise: 'Then we your people, the flock of your pasture, will give thanks to you forever; from generation to generation we will recount your praise.'
1.historical_context: The psalm most naturally fits the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC, though some read it in the context of the Maccabean period (1 Macc 7:17 quotes vv. 2–3). The unburied bodies of vv. 2–3 reflect the Deuteronomic covenant curse of Deut 28:26 — 'your dead body shall be food for all birds of the air' — making the disaster legible as covenant judgment. The 'seven times' vengeance of v. 12 — 'Return sevenfold into the lap of our neighbors the taunts with which they have taunted you, O Lord!' — uses the covenant-curse number of Lev 26:18.
1.literary_structure: The psalm moves through three rhetorical phases: lament of the catastrophe (vv. 1–4) → appeal based on YHWH's jealousy and Israel's iniquity (vv. 5–8) → appeal based on YHWH's own glory and honor (vv. 9–13). The progression is from description to petition to argument, with the ultimate ground of the appeal being YHWH's name rather than Israel's worthiness. The closing vow of praise (v. 13) is the communal covenant commitment that the deliverance being requested will be met with perpetual thanksgiving.
1.theological_application: The 'God of our salvation' address of v. 9 — 'for the glory of your name... for your name's sake' — establishes the deepest ground of prayer: appeal to YHWH's own glory and reputation. When human merit is bankrupt and the situation desperate, the most theologically grounded petition is for YHWH to act for the sake of his own name. This is not manipulation but alignment: the psalmist is asking YHWH to be consistent with his own declared character. Daniel prays the same way in Dan 9:17–19: 'for your own sake... for your own sake, O my God.'
1.christology: The atonement petition of v. 9 — 'atone for our sins, for your name's sake' — uses the same kippēr word (atone/cover) that lies behind the entire sacrificial system. The NT announces that YHWH has answered this prayer definitively in Christ: 'God put forward [Christ] as a propitiation by his blood, to be received by faith. This was to show God's righteousness... so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus' (Rom 3:25–26). The atonement for which Psalm 79 cried out has been made — for YHWH's own name's sake, in Christ.
